_meta.description: Two-unit financial literacy course covering budgeting and needs vs wants for young learners.
nextjs_notes: Import as a static JSON or fetch from /api/courses/financial-verticals.
VideoPlayer props: { title, youtube_url, points, audio_location, voice_text }
SubmissionCard props: { prompt, voice_text, emoji_options[], reference_submissions[], audio_location }
QuizQuestion props: { question, voice_text, options[], correct_option, audio_location }

QuizFeedback props (shown on wrong answer): { consolation, consolation_voice_text, audio_location_consolation, audio_location_cheer_up, hint, hint_voice_text, audio_location_hint, explanation, explanation_voice_text, audio_location_explanation }
voice_text fields are the spoken-aloud script for their sibling text field, written naturally for TTS.
audio_location_cheer_up is a short distinct pep-talk clip played after the consolation voice line and before the hint is revealed.
After a quiz finishes, post { unit_id, q1_correct, q2_correct, q3_correct } to /api/progress.
After a submission, post { unit_id, emoji_chosen } to /api/submissions.

units.unit_name: Budget Smart — Money Gets a Plan

video.title: Can Ravi Save ₹50? 💰
video.description: Follow Ravi as he figures out where his pocket money is going — and makes a smart plan to save more. You'll build your own budget by the end!
video.voice_text: Quick — has this ever happened to you? You get money, you're excited, you spend it on snacks and fun stuff — and then you need something actually important and there's nothing left? Yep, that's happened to pretty much everyone. But there's a way out, and it's called budgeting. It sounds fancy but it's really just making a plan for your money before you spend it. Ravi's about to show us how — let's see if he can pull it off.

submission.prompt: Build your own weekly budget! Imagine your income is ₹500 and your savings goal is ₹150. List your needs and wants and fill in the budget table. Click a photo and share it! 💰
submission.voice_text: Alright, now it's your turn to be the budget boss! Imagine you have ₹500 for the week and you want to save ₹150. First, decide your savings goal and set that aside. Then figure out what you need — school stuff, transport — and what you want — snacks, fun things. Make a table in your notebook: Income, Savings, Needs, Wants. Fill it in and make sure the numbers actually add up. Then snap a photo and show us your money plan!

reference_submissions.title: Riya's Budget Table
reference_submissions.description: A neat table with Income ₹500, Savings ₹150, Needs ₹220 (notebook, bus fare), Wants ₹130 (snacks, stickers). Total checks out perfectly.

reference_submissions.title: Kiran's Colour-Coded Budget
reference_submissions.description: Needs highlighted in green, Wants in yellow, Savings in blue. Clear, organised, and maths is correct.

reference_submissions.title: Sahil's Detailed Plan
reference_submissions.description: Each item listed with its cost, then grouped into Needs and Wants, with a final Savings row showing ₹150 remaining. Great detail!

questions.question: What is a budget?
questions.voice_text: First question — in your own words after watching the video, what is a budget?

options.label: A list of games you like
A plan for using money wisely
A box to keep your money in

questions.explanation: A budget is a plan — it helps you decide in advance where your money will go. With a budget, you make sure the important things get paid for first, and you don't run out of money at the wrong moment.
questions.explanation_voice_text: A budget is a plan for using your money wisely. It's how you decide in advance — before you've spent anything — where each rupee goes. That way, when you need money for something important, you actually have it. A box or a games list can't do that for you!
questions.consolation: Not quite — think about what Ravi made in the video!
questions.consolation_voice_text: Not quite — go back to what Ravi made in the video. He sat down with a table and figured out his money. What was that called?
questions.hint: Think about what the word 'plan' has to do with money — a budget helps you plan where your money will go.
questions.hint_voice_text: Here's your clue: a budget is all about planning. Before you spend, you decide where your money goes. Which option sounds like a plan?

questions.question: Which of these is a Need?
questions.voice_text: Question two — this one is about spotting the difference. Out of these three, which one is a Need?
questions.explanation: A notebook for school is a Need — you genuinely require it to study and learn. Ice cream and movie tickets are Wants — enjoyable, but you can live and do well without them.
questions.explanation_voice_text: A school notebook is a Need! You need it to study, take notes, and do your work. Ice cream is delicious, and movies are fun — but if you didn't have them, your life would continue just fine. That's the difference between a Need and a Want.
questions.consolation: Hmm, not quite — ask yourself: could you survive school without it?
questions.consolation_voice_text: Hmm, not quite! Here's the test: could you get through your school day without it? If yes, it's probably a Want. If no, it might be a Need.
questions.hint: A Need is something essential — something you genuinely can't do without. Which of these three do you actually need for school?
questions.hint_voice_text: Here's your hint: a Need is something you can't really do without — especially for school or daily life. Which one of those options would cause a real problem if you didn't have it?

questions.question: Your income is ₹200 and you want to save ₹50. How much can you spend?
questions.voice_text: Last question — a little maths! Your income is two hundred rupees and your savings goal is fifty. What's left for spending?
questions.explanation: ₹200 income minus ₹50 savings = ₹150 to spend. Simple but powerful — once you take out your savings first, you know exactly how much is available for everything else.
questions.explanation_voice_text: Two hundred minus fifty equals one hundred and fifty. So you have ₹150 to spend on your needs and wants. The key habit here is saving first — pull out your ₹50 right away so you're never tempted to spend it, and then use the remaining ₹150 wisely.
questions.consolation: Not quite — try the maths: income minus savings!
questions.consolation_voice_text: Not quite! Just do the subtraction: take your savings goal away from your total income. What's left is what you can spend.
questions.hint: Spend = Income minus Savings. So ₹200 minus ₹50 equals...?
questions.hint_voice_text: Here's the formula: what you can spend equals your income minus your savings goal. So take two hundred and subtract fifty — what do you get?

units.unit_name: Needs vs Wants — Smart Choices Start Here

video.title: Can Needs Beat Your Wants? 🛒
video.description: Meet the Gupta family and find out how understanding Needs and Wants can help them — and you — save more and stress less.
video.voice_text: Imagine you're locked in a room with two doors. Behind one door: food and water. Behind the other: pizza and cold drink. You can only open one. Which do you pick? I think we both know the answer — but here's the interesting question: why? Today we're going to meet the Gupta family, and they're going to help us understand exactly what separates a Need from a Want — and how knowing that difference can change the way you use money forever.

submission.prompt: Create a Needs vs Wants table in your notebook! Write at least 3 Needs and 3 Wants from your own life. Draw a small icon next to each one. Share a photo! 🛒
submission.voice_text: Time to be a money detective — about your own life! Make a two-column table in your notebook: Needs on the left, Wants on the right. Think about your own week and fill in at least three things in each column. Then draw a tiny icon next to each one — a pencil, a snack, whatever it is. When you're done, take a photo and share it. I want to see the real stuff from your real life!

emoji_options.label: I had to think about some

reference_submissions.title: Aanya's Table
reference_submissions.description: Needs: school bag, lunch, uniform. Wants: stickers, video game, toy. Each with a cute hand-drawn icon. Perfect!

reference_submissions.title: Rohan's Detailed List
reference_submissions.description: Five Needs and five Wants, with a short reason written next to each one explaining why it's a Need or Want. Extra effort, great thinking.

reference_submissions.title: Preet's Colourful Chart
reference_submissions.description: A bright two-column table with green for Needs and orange for Wants. Icons are clear and the sorting is accurate.

questions.question: What does 'Income' mean?
questions.voice_text: First question — this is a vocabulary one. What does the word income actually mean?
questions.explanation: Income is the money you earn — from a job, from pocket money given as a reward, or from any work you do. It's what comes IN to your hands before you start spending.
questions.explanation_voice_text: Income is the money that comes in — money you earn by working, or pocket money you receive as a reward. It's not money you borrow or money you spend. Think of it as your starting point before you make any decisions about where the money goes.
questions.consolation: Not quite — income is what comes IN to you, not what goes out!
questions.consolation_voice_text: Not quite — here's a tip: the word 'income' has the word 'in' right at the start. It's money that comes IN to you. What kind of money comes in?
questions.hint: Think about Mr. Gupta and Mrs. Gupta from the video — they both went to work. The money they received for their work is their income.
questions.hint_voice_text: Think about the Gupta family. Mr. Gupta teaches, Mrs. Gupta stitches clothes. The money they get in return for doing that work — that's their income. Which option matches that?

questions.question: You have ₹50. Medicine costs ₹40 and chips cost ₹20. What should you buy first?
questions.voice_text: Question two — you've got fifty rupees, not quite enough for both. You have to choose. What do you do?
questions.explanation: Medicine is a Need — your health comes first. Chips are a Want — enjoyable but not essential. When money is limited, Needs always take priority over Wants. You can skip chips; you can't skip medicine.
questions.explanation_voice_text: Medicine first, every time. Your health is a Need — not having medicine when you're sick is a real problem. Chips are a Want — they're tasty but you won't be harmed without them. When money is tight, Needs win. That's the rule, and it's a good one.
questions.consolation: Think about what actually matters more here — your health or a snack!
questions.consolation_voice_text: Take a moment and think: one of those things is important for your health, and one is just nice to have. Which one actually matters if you're not feeling well?
questions.hint: Ask yourself: which of these two things would cause a real problem if you didn't have it?
questions.hint_voice_text: Here's the question to ask: what happens if you don't get each one? If you don't get chips, you're just a bit disappointed. If you don't get medicine when you're sick — that's a real problem. Which should come first?

questions.question: Which of these is responsible money behaviour?
questions.voice_text: Last question — which of these shows someone who really understands money?

options.label: Spending most of your money on video games and movies
Buying expensive things just to look cool
Thinking about your Needs before spending on Wants

questions.explanation: Thinking about Needs before Wants is the responsible choice. It doesn't mean never enjoying Wants — it just means being intentional. Plan first, spend second. That's how you avoid running out of money when it matters most.
questions.explanation_voice_text: Thinking about your Needs before spending on Wants is the smart move. It doesn't mean you can never enjoy things — it just means you plan first. Needs covered? Great. Money left over? Now you can enjoy your Wants without worry. That's what responsible money behaviour looks like.
questions.consolation: Think about what would actually serve you best in the long run!
questions.consolation_voice_text: Think about which of those choices you'd thank yourself for a week later. Which one keeps you from being stuck without money when something important comes up?
questions.hint: Responsible money behaviour is about planning and prioritising — making sure the important things are covered before the fun things.
questions.hint_voice_text: Here's your clue: responsible money behaviour means thinking ahead. You want to make sure the important things are always covered, and then enjoy the fun things with what's left. Which option shows that?
